### Title

Correlation Between Average Token Entropy and Character Error Rate

### Purpose of the Figure

Provides an alternative view on the correlation between average token entropy and character error rate to [figure_04.md](figure_04.md) using pearson and spearman metrics. Each quartile contains a small number of high-entropy, high-CER pages that strongly drive Pearson but not Spearman. Spearman is a better correlation metric that reflects the trend across the full distribution.

### Generated By

[stratified_analysis.py](../../scripts/stratified_analysis.py)

### Input Files Used

[results_k_10.csv](../../results/csv/results_k_10.csv)

### Output Filenames

PNG: [figure_05_stratified_correlations.png](../../results/figures/figure_05_stratified_correlations.png)

SVG: [figure_05_stratified_correlations.svg](../../results/figures/figure_05_stratified_correlations.svg)

### X-axis

Ground-Truth Length (Characters) Quartile

### Y-axis

Correlation

### Units

Correlation (blue bar): Pearson

Correlation (orange bar): Spearman

### Other Information

Abbreviations: 'n' refers to the number of samples within each quartile and 'QX' refers to the quartile where X indicates the quartile.

Filtering rules: samples with a CER > 2% were filtered from the dataset.   

Normalization choices: whitespaces were normalized, punctuation was removed entirely, and all characters were converted to lowercase between ground-truth and ocr texts.

Sample size: the plot consists of 100 randomized page excerpts stratified into four quartiles.

### Figure Importance

Shows how the Pearson correlation coefficient is sensitive to the small number of high entropy, high CER values samples within each quartile, and how Spearman is a better metric to use here.
